title: Differentially Private Fine-tuning of Language Models

structured.summary: The paper presents a meta-framework for differentially private fine-tuning of large pretrained language models by training only a small set of additional parameters using parameter-efficient methods like LoRA and Adapters while freezing the base weights. This approach reduces noise injection, computational overhead, and memory usage, achieving near non-private performance on multiple NLP tasks and scaling to very large models such as GPT-2-XL.

strengths.point: Significant improvement in privacy-utility tradeoff using parameter-efficient tuning under DP
strengths.evidence: Achieves near non-private accuracy on GLUE tasks and strong BLEU/ROUGE scores on generation benchmarks with ε≈6-7, outperforming prior full fine-tuning DP methods.

strengths.point: Scalability to very large models with reduced memory and compute
strengths.evidence: Demonstrates first DP fine-tuning results on GPT-2-XL (1.5B parameters) with low-rank methods using much less GPU memory and training time than full DPSGD.

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical evaluation across multiple datasets, model sizes, and parameter-efficient techniques
strengths.evidence: Experiments on RoBERTa-base/large and GPT-2 small to XL, with LoRA, Adapters, and Compacter, show consistent gains and robustness.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient comparison to well-tuned full DPSGD fine-tuning baselines
weaknesses.evidence: Mentions concurrent work but lacks detailed experiments contrasting DP LoRA against carefully tuned full DPSGD across tasks and model sizes.

weaknesses.point: Lack of privacy leakage or membership inference attack evaluation
weaknesses.evidence: Evaluation only measures standard utility metrics (accuracy, BLEU, ROUGE) without empirical tests of privacy leakage beyond DP parameters.

weaknesses.point: Missing detailed hyperparameter and training configuration disclosures
weaknesses.evidence: Key DP training hyperparameters like noise multiplier, clipping norm, batch size, and iteration counts are not fully tabulated, limiting reproducibility.

weaknesses.point: No analysis or quantification of privacy amplification from freezing pretrained weights
weaknesses.evidence: Claims freezing base weights reduces noise and improves privacy-utility tradeoff but lacks theoretical or empirical justification.

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation of privacy budgets and no exploration of stricter DP regimes
weaknesses.evidence: Most results focus on ε≈6-7; no performance trends shown for lower ε values or broader privacy settings.

weaknesses.point: No discussion or evaluation of out-of-distribution generalization limitations
weaknesses.evidence: Freezing pretrained weights might reduce adaptability but paper omits such analysis.
Architecture diagrams lack detailed labeling; mixing of notation for pretrained and fine-tuned functions is inconsistent and potentially confusing.

weaknesses.point: No public release of code or pretrained DP parameter-efficient models
weaknesses.evidence: Paper does not mention code or checkpoint availability, hindering verification and adoption.

questions.question: How does DP LoRA compare to carefully tuned full DPSGD fine-tuning across diverse tasks and model scales?
questions.motivation: To establish the practical advantage and novelty of parameter-efficient DP tuning over full fine-tuning baselines.

questions.question: What is the empirical privacy leakage risk beyond ε,δ guarantees, e.g., via membership inference or extraction attacks?
questions.motivation: To validate the robustness of the proposed DP fine-tuning beyond theoretical privacy accounting.

questions.question: Can the approach generalize to non-English languages, other modalities, or real-world sensitive domains like medical NLP?
questions.motivation: To assess the broader applicability and impact of the method across privacy-critical applications.

questions.question: Is there a measurable privacy amplification effect from freezing pretrained weights, and can it be theoretically characterized?
questions.motivation: To support the claimed privacy-utility benefits of freezing weights with rigorous analysis.

critique_points.point_001: Insufficient comparison to well-tuned full DPSGD fine-tuning baselines. Mentions concurrent work but lacks detailed experiments contrasting DP LoRA against carefully tuned full DPSGD across tasks and model sizes
critique_points.point_002: Lack of privacy leakage or membership inference attack evaluation. Evaluation only measures standard utility metrics (accuracy, BLEU, ROUGE) without empirical tests of privacy leakage beyond DP parameters
critique_points.point_003: Missing detailed hyperparameter and training configuration disclosures. Key DP training hyperparameters like noise multiplier, clipping norm, batch size, and iteration counts are not fully tabulated, limiting reproducibility
critique_points.point_004: No analysis or quantification of privacy amplification from freezing pretrained weights. Claims freezing base weights reduces noise and improves privacy-utility tradeoff but lacks theoretical or empirical justification
critique_points.point_005: Limited evaluation of privacy budgets and no exploration of stricter DP regimes. Most results focus on ε≈6-7; no performance trends shown for lower ε values or broader privacy settings
critique_points.point_006: No discussion or evaluation of out-of-distribution generalization limitations. Freezing pretrained weights might reduce adaptability but paper omits such analysis
critique_points.point_007: Notation and presentation clarity issues. Architecture diagrams lack detailed labeling; mixing of notation for pretrained and fine-tuned functions is inconsistent and potentially confusing
critique_points.point_008: No public release of code or pretrained DP parameter-efficient models. Paper does not mention code or checkpoint availability, hindering verification and adoption
